beats.activity: Maintains drilling equipment. Notes instruments behaving oddly
Triangular markings in wrong strata. Equipment fails near certain sites
Finds Lake's private journal. Recognizes his own drill marks on destroyed equipment
beats.mcNote: Pabodie recognizes his own drill marks on destroyed equipment — the 1930 expedition was here.
beats.npcBehavior: Runs fingers over drill marks. 'These are mine. I made these cuts three years ago. But something cut them back.'

beats.activity: Notes dogs' behavior changes as biological anomaly
The triangular markings contain biological material that shouldn't exist
The surviving specimen defies every taxonomy she knows
beats.mcNote: The specimen defies every taxonomy. This is where Bryce's confidence begins to crack.
beats.npcBehavior: Stares at the specimen for a full minute before speaking. 'This isn't wrong. I'm wrong. Everything I know is insufficient.'

beats.activity: Notes dogs' distress. Checks perimeter out of habit
Night watch — hears sounds the wind doesn't explain
Reads the destruction at Lake's camp as combat, not accident
beats.mcNote: Rourke reads the destruction as a combat scene, not an accident. Military eye sees what civilians miss.
beats.npcBehavior: Kneels by the wreckage. 'This wasn't weather. This was organized violence. Something fought here and won.'

beats.activity: Photographs the mirages. Interviews crew about previous voyages
Documents everything. This is bigger than the Innsmouth story
Photographs Lake's camp. Reads his journal. The story of a lifetime
beats.mcNote: This is where Caldwell's journalist instinct becomes dangerous — she wants closer, not safer.
beats.npcBehavior: Photographs everything twice. 'Lake's journal ends mid-sentence. That's not how scientists write. That's how men interrupted by something write.'

beats.activity: Preps the planes for Antarctic conditions. First odd readings
Equipment failures near certain sites. Nothing he fixes stays fixed
Recognizes deliberate damage to Lake's equipment. Not weather — intent
beats.mcNote: Equipment destroyed by something that doesn't use tools. Gedney's rational world takes its first hit.
beats.npcBehavior: Picks up a shattered gauge. 'This wasn't dropped. It was crushed. But there are no tool marks. What crushes steel without tools?'

beats.activity: Radio clear. Regular check-ins with Moore. First hints of unusual static
Static patterns that don't match any known interference. The radio 'breathes'
Radio reception degrades near Lake's camp. Something interferes
beats.mcNote: Radio begins to degrade here. Webb notices the pattern before anyone else.
beats.npcBehavior: Adjusts the receiver. 'Moore's signal is clear from base camp. Here, it... pulses. Like something is between us and the antenna.'

beats.activity: Tracks expenses. Questions delays. Time is money
The anomalies have commercial implications. Begins second ledger
A specimen that defies classification is worth more than the entire expedition
beats.mcNote: Price sees dollar signs in the specimens. His greed is about to become a plot driver.
beats.npcBehavior: Makes notes in the second ledger. 'The biological material alone is worth... considerable. Starkweather will want to know.'

beats.activity: Regular coded reports to Moore. Notes anomalies objectively
Coded report to Moore on the markings. Moore demands caution
Tries to report to Moore. Radio increasingly unreliable. Evidence is undeniable
beats.mcNote: Her geology training says these formations are impossible. Her coded report to Moore says 'anomalous.'
beats.npcBehavior: Samples the rock face. 'These strata are Pre-Cambrian. Nothing should be in Pre-Cambrian strata. And yet.'

beats.activity: The ice is wrong. Mirages persist too long. The dogs know
Recalls Lars Larsen's crew seeing stone ruins from a crow's nest in 1929
Reads the ice around Lake's camp. Something melted it from below
beats.mcNote: The old sailor's tales are becoming real. Sørensen is the first to accept what others deny.
beats.npcBehavior: Goes quiet. Touches the ice wall. 'A man from Lars Larsen's crew told me about stone ruins. I called him a drunk. I owe him an apology.'

beats.activity: The dogs are uneasy. Dismissed as weather — but McReady knows dogs
Dogs refuse food. Won't face south. They know something is there
No dog remains at Lake's camp. The dogs either fled or were taken
beats.mcNote: The dogs won't approach the camp. McReady knows their instincts are better than his instruments.
beats.npcBehavior: Holds the lead dog back. 'She won't go closer. Last time I saw dogs this scared was when a glacier calved. They knew before anyone.'

beats.activity: The mirages match descriptions in the Pnakotic Manuscripts. The wind piping matches too
The markings correlate with Eltdown Shards. Three notebooks filling fast
Lake's journal confirms Dyer's private account. The pattern is real
beats.mcNote: Angell's texts are being confirmed in real time. This should feel vindicating but feels like doom.
beats.npcBehavior: Hands trembling, cross-references a notebook. 'This matches the Pnakotic fragments. Word for word. I was right. God help me, I was right.'

beats.activity: The dogs sense it. In the islands, the animals always knew first
The wind piping matches descriptions from Solomon Islands elders
These people died because they didn't listen to the warnings. As always
beats.mcNote: The indigenous warnings Waverly heard in the Solomons described this exact scenario.
beats.npcBehavior: Closes her eyes. 'In the Solomons, they told me about a place where the old things sleep. They said: do not wake them. We have woken them.'

beats.activity: Paces the deck. Points at the mirages as proof. 'Dyer described exactly this'
The markings confirm Dyer. Starkweather is electric — 'I told you. I told all of you'
Lake's camp is exactly as Dyer described. The vindication tastes different now — because Dyer also described what came next
beats.mcNote: This is the hinge. Starkweather wanted to be right. Now he IS right — and the implications are landing. His emotional shift from vindication to unease should be visible but not yet acknowledged by him. He's still pushing forward, but the swagger has a crack in it.
beats.npcBehavior: Still asserting authority but the orders come faster, with less patience. Snaps at Price when questioned about costs. Insists on examining the specimen personally. Doesn't sleep.

beats.activity: Regular radio check-ins. Demands progress reports. Dismisses mirage sightings as atmospheric refraction
Last clear radio contact with full context. The markings intrigue him. Demands samples, measurements, photographs. 'Interesting if verified'
beats.mcNote: This is the last time Moore's voice comes through clearly. The full weight of scientific authority, 10,000 miles away. He's genuinely interested in the triangular markings but frames everything as geology. Make his voice clear and strong here — it will fade.
beats.npcBehavior: Rapid-fire questions through the radio. Cross-references with known geological formations. Instructs Ainsworth to take independent measurements. 'Don't let Starkweather's enthusiasm contaminate the data.' Ends with: 'Good work. Report again at 0600.'

beats.activity: Radio degrades. Catches fragments of Lake's camp report. Demands data through static. 'Say again — specimen? What specimen?'
beats.mcNote: Moore's voice breaks up here. He catches enough to be alarmed but not enough to understand. The skeptic is losing his primary tool — information. His frustration should carry through the static. He's used to controlling this from afar, and the control is slipping.
beats.npcBehavior: Repeats 'say again' through static. When he catches the word 'specimen,' his tone changes — sharper, more urgent. 'Do NOT touch anything until Ainsworth confirms— [static].' The connection dies mid-sentence.
